subtext: Pick the closest — this shapes which questions we ask next.
why: How you sell changes almost everything else we ask — permits, costs and competitors differ between markets, wholesale and online.

option_notes.Weekend markets / pop-ups: We'll include market stall fees, vendor permits and what similar stalls charge in your area.
option_notes.Wholesale to local shops: We'll look at wholesale margins, minimum order quantities and how to approach local retailers.
option_notes.Own online store: We'll focus on platform costs, shipping and how to get your first online customers — no local permits needed.
option_notes.Marketplace (Etsy / eBay): We'll cover marketplace fees, listing optimisation and how buyers there discover and compare products.
option_notes.Mix of local + online: We'll cover both local set-up costs and online selling costs, since you're splitting effort across channels.

subtext: Optional — helps us find local competitors, pricing and set-up costs for your area. e.g. Brisbane, QLD.

in: Weekend markets / pop-ups
Wholesale to local shops
Mix of local + online

why: Local pricing, permits and competitor research are sharper with a city or region than with just a country.

subtext: This decides which cost questions we ask next — self-made and manufactured routes are priced very differently.
options: I'll make it myself at home
I'll make it in a rented commercial space
A local manufacturer will make it for me
An overseas manufacturer will make it for me
why: Who makes your product decides which cost questions follow, and whether your report covers permits and home-business rules or unit costs, minimum orders and compliance.

option_notes.I'll make it myself at home: We'll check home-business and food/safety permit rules for your area, and keep your cost base low with no rent or fit-out to budget for.
option_notes.I'll make it in a rented commercial spac: We'll factor in commercial rent, fit-out costs and the compliance rules that come with a dedicated premises — a bigger cost base than home.
option_notes.A local manufacturer will make it for me: We'll cover per-unit costs, minimum order quantities, and getting samples made and quality-checked before a full production run.
option_notes.An overseas manufacturer will make it fo: We'll cover per-unit costs, minimum order quantities, samples and quality control, plus freight and import duties to get stock to you.

text: Who or what is this product specifically for?
subtext: e.g., 'dogs with anxiety', 'coffee drinkers who like fruity notes', 'cyclists commuting in the rain'.
why: The more specific your target customer, the sharper we can make the marketing and demand research in your report.

text: List your main ingredients or materials with rough quantities per batch.
subtext: Free text is fine — e.g., '500g oat flour, 2 eggs, 400g pumpkin, 200g peanut butter' or '500g soy wax, 200ml fragrance oil, 40 wicks'.

in: I'll make it myself at home
I'll make it in a rented commercial space

why: Your ingredient list drives the cost-per-batch numbers in your report. Rough numbers are fine — the report refines these with real local prices.

text: Which equipment do you already own?
subtext: Tick everything you have. Anything not ticked, we'll assume you need to buy.

in: I'll make it myself at home
I'll make it in a rented commercial space

why: This tells us your realistic starting costs — equipment you already own doesn't need to be bought again.

text: Total wattage of the main heat/power equipment you'll run per batch?
subtext: Check the label on your oven, dehydrator or wax melter. If unsure, a domestic oven is ~1800–2400W.

in: I'll make it myself at home
I'll make it in a rented commercial space

why: Power draw feeds your running-cost estimate per batch. Rough numbers are fine — the report refines these with real local prices.

text: How many minutes of hands-on work per batch?
subtext: Only the time you're actually mixing, shaping, packaging — not oven time.

in: I'll make it myself at home
I'll make it in a rented commercial space

why: Hands-on time is what your hourly rate gets multiplied by — it's a core input to your unit economics.

text: How many finished units come out of one batch?
subtext: e.g., '40 packs of treats', '24 bottles of sauce', '24 candles'.

in: I'll make it myself at home
I'll make it in a rented commercial space

why: How many units come out of a batch is what spreads your costs across — it's central to your per-unit price.

text: What's the hourly rate you want to pay yourself for hands-on work?
subtext: In your local currency. If you're not sure, pick a number you'd accept for a casual job locally.

in: I'll make it myself at home
I'll make it in a rented commercial space

why: What you pay yourself per hour is a core input to your numbers. Example figures here are just examples — your report prices everything in your own currency, using costs for your country.

text: What will the manufacturer charge you per unit — if you already know?
subtext: Optional. Leave blank and we'll estimate manufacturing costs in your report — a real quote just makes the numbers sharper.

in: A local manufacturer will make it for me
An overseas manufacturer will make it for me

why: Per-unit cost is the base of your margins; we'll estimate it if blank. Example figures here are just examples — your report prices everything in your own currency, using costs for your country.

text: How much can you put into your first production run?
subtext: Include minimum order quantities, samples and any tooling costs.

in: A local manufacturer will make it for me
An overseas manufacturer will make it for me

why: This covers minimum order quantities, samples and tooling, and sets what scale of first run the report recommends. Figures are just examples — priced in your own currency.

subtext: This changes what your report budgets for and what it assumes you've already paid for.
options: A prototype or sample in hand
I already have a manufacturer quote

in: A local manufacturer will make it for me
An overseas manufacturer will make it for me

why: How far along you are changes what your report budgets for next — from design and prototyping costs through to quotes you already have in hand.

option_notes.Just the idea: We'll budget for design, prototyping and getting your first sample made from scratch.
option_notes.Sketches or a 3D design: We'll budget for turning your design into a physical prototype or sample, then production.
option_notes.A prototype or sample in hand: We won't charge you again for prototyping — your report picks up from getting it manufactured at scale.
option_notes.I already have a manufacturer quote: We'll use your quote as the cost baseline and focus the report on margins, first-run budget and launch.

text: Roughly what will packaging and delivery cost per unit?
subtext: Optional — most people don't know this yet. Leave blank and the report estimates it.

in: A local manufacturer will make it for me
An overseas manufacturer will make it for me

why: Packaging and delivery are part of your per-unit cost. Rough numbers are fine — the report refines these with real local prices.
